request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"rethinkdb::devtools","name":"rethinkdb","one_liner":"Free, open-source distributed document database with real-time changefeeds, built for developers, that failed to monetize its OSS traction before shutting down in 2016.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"The pitch is a paid B2B SaaS (hosted control plane with CLI) — a clear commercial product from day one. RethinkDB was free/open-source with no meaningful commercial layer; its monetization model is listed as 'other' with a free price point. The structural difference is large, though both target developer-buyers."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both serve the developer infrastructure market and rely on developer adoption as the primary growth vector. However RethinkDB targeted the database layer (distributed document store), while the pitch targets ephemeral preview environments — a CI/CD and platform-engineering niche. Adjacent markets, not the same one."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both use a developer-first, bottoms-up GTM: win individual developers or small teams through a CLI/open tooling experience, then expand. RethinkDB relied on OSS community momentum; the pitch uses a CLI plus hosted plane, a hybrid of the same pattern. The mechanism is similar even if the commercialization wrapper differs."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"RethinkDB operated for ~7 years (2009-2016) with YC seed funding and scaled to a notable OSS community but never achieved sustainable revenue scale. The pitch is presumably early-stage. Both start at the same developer-adoption seed phase, though RethinkDB's final scale was larger than a typical seed startup."}},"why_similar":"Both target developers as the primary customer, both use a CLI-centric, bottoms-up adoption motion, and both sit in the developer-infrastructure space. RethinkDB's core lesson — that developer love and OSS popularity do not automatically convert to paying customers — is directly applicable to a pitch that includes a free CLI entry point alongside a hosted paid plane. The challenge of converting enthusiastic developers into enterprise or team-level revenue is the central thread connecting both companies.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization model: The pitch is explicitly B2B SaaS with a hosted paid control plane from the start; RethinkDB was free/open-source with price_point listed as 'free' and no commercial tier that generated meaningful revenue. 2. Product domain: RethinkDB was a distributed document database; the pitch is an ephemeral preview-environment orchestration tool — a DevOps/platform-engineering product, not a data-persistence product. 3. Revenue intent: The pitch embeds monetization (hosted service) into the core product architecture, whereas RethinkDB's commercial strategy was never clearly operationalized alongside its OSS distribution.","failure_causes":["inability to monetize open-source adoption","free price point with no sustainable commercial tier","developer enthusiasm not converted to enterprise revenue","competition from better-funded database incumbents","no clear path from OSS community to paying customers","prolonged pre-revenue runway exhaustion"],"lessons_for_input":["Gate the hosted control plane behind a paid tier early — never let the free CLI become the only value delivery mechanism or you will replicate RethinkDB's monetization void.","Define your paid conversion funnel before launch: know exactly which CLI users should upgrade to the hosted plane and what triggers that upgrade.","Track revenue-correlated metrics (teams created, environments provisioned per paying org) from day one, not just developer signups or GitHub stars.","Establish enterprise or mid-market contracts as a parallel motion alongside bottoms-up developer adoption — OSS/CLI traction alone will not sustain the business.","Avoid over-investing in feature breadth (RethinkDB built an entire query language) before proving willingness-to-pay; ship the paid hosted plane fast and iterate."]}
